w:r.w:t: A Gamified Digital Platform for Data Collection and Teaching of the Critically Endangered Toto Language
Department of Humanities and Social Sciences, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, India

), spoken by approximately 1,600 people in Totopara, West Bengal, India, is critically endangered. Although a Toto script was created in 2015 and accepted into Unicode in 2021, no interactive digital tools exist for learning or documenting the language. We present a publicly deployed, gamified web platform that integrates bilingual audio recordings collected from native speakers, vocabulary organised across six everyday domains, illustrated concept stories with slide-based narration, traditional folk stories with cultural annotations, and age-appropriate quizzes. This paper describes the field-based data collection methodology, the three-layer web architecture built on React and Supabase, and the experience-point gamification system designed to sustain learner engagement. The platform is live at
Toto language, endangered language preservation, gamification, language learning, Tibeto-Burman, data collection, bilingual content

When a language disappears, it takes with it cen- turies of accumulated knowledge about the natural world, social relationships, and ways of thinking that no other language expresses in quite the same way (
). UNESCO estimates that roughly 40% of the world’s 7,000 languages may vanish within this century (
), and the loss is especially acute for small communities whose entire cultural memory is encoded in a single tongue.

India is home to extraordinary linguistic diversity, yet modern language technology has overwhelm- ingly favoured a handful of well-resourced languages. Data-driven techniques such as deep learning have produced impressive results for English and Hindi, but they remain out of reach for the vast majority of Tibeto-Burman and Austro-Asiatic languages, which lack even basic digital corpora (
, is one such language. A Tibeto-Burman language of the Himalayan sub- family (
), it is spoken by roughly 1,600 people in the village of Totopara, Alipurduar district, West Bengal (
(Refer to 2011 Census of India, not Sanyal
. In 2015, community leader Dhani- ram Toto devised an alphabetic script for the lan- guage, and in 2021 that script was accepted into Unicode (
). These are important milestones, but none of them offer an interactive way for children or adults to actually
Commercial platforms such as Duolingo have be- gun to serve minority languages like Hawaiian and Navajo (
), and community-driven projects like FirstVoices support dozens of indigenous language communities in British Columbia (
these platforms depend on large user bases and pre- existing digital infrastructure that a community of 1,600 speakers simply does not have.

To address this gap, we built and deployed a gamified web platform for learning Toto. The plat- form serves simultaneously as a pedagogical tool and a data-collection instrument: every word card, story, and quiz item is backed by audio recorded in the field with native speakers. In the sections that follow we describe how the data was collected (Sec- tion
), and where we plan to take it next (Section
Several projects have built speech corpora for Indian languages, including the IIITH-ILSC database (
). For less-resourced languages, Basu et al. (
) compiled corpora for Awadhi, Bhojpuri, Braj, and Magahi, while Deka et al. (
) created speech corpora for languages of North-East India, and Sarma et al. (
Closer to our focus, the SpeeD-IL project (
) collected speech data for several Tibeto-Burman languages including Toto, though its goal is au- tomatic speech recognition rather than language teaching. Saha et al. (
) explored morphological analysis for a Toto-Bangla-English trilingual appli- cation, and historical documentation goes back to Grierson (
) provides a collaborative online hub for sharing resources on
) developed an adaptive, game-based language-learning system for Irish. Research consistently shows that gamification boosts learner motivation and engagement (
) explored AI-powered gamification specifically for endangered- language contexts.
What is largely missing from this landscape is a
, community-facing tool that combines field data collection with interactive learning for a critically endangered language. Our platform is designed to fill exactly that gap.

This entire section should flow with Paragraph 3 of the Introduction. Heading 3 could be Methodology under which you will have to mention Interview method (semi structured) and then describe the questionnaire.
Totopara lies in Madarihat, Alipurduar district, close to the Bhutan border. The village comprises six hamlets: Mondalgaon, Dumsinggon, Pujagaon, Subhapara, Panchayatpara, and Mitranggaon (
). The Toto language has six vowels, nineteen conso- nants, and a two-way tonal contrast (

). Because the community remains relatively isolated, its vo- cabulary is closely tied to traditional life; during fieldwork we identified a number of modern con- cepts (e.g. “law”, “celebration”, “mirror”) that have no Toto equivalent and were excluded from the learning content.
We drew on three established approaches from doc- umentary linguistics:
Native speakers listen to an English word or sentence and record the Toto equivalent.
Speakers narrate sto- ries prompted by pictures or scenarios, produc- ing the concept-story content.
Open conversations with elders and younger speakers yielded folk stories, cultural knowledge, and natural vocab- ulary.
). All audio was captured in 16-bit PCM at 16 kHz, a format compatible with standard speech-processing toolkits.
Words are organised into six everyday domains:
. Each domain was chosen to reflect the communica- tive needs and cultural context of the Toto commu- nity rather than an externally imposed taxonomy. Figure
shows the browsing interface that allows learners to filter words by category.
Figure 1: The word-browsing interface. Users filter by category, view bilingual word cards, and play native-speaker audio in both Toto and English.
Figure 2: The landing page with role-based entry points for learners and educators.
The platform is a single-page web application or- ganised in three layers:
React 18 with TypeScript, Tail- wind CSS for styling, and the shadcn/ui compo- nent library for accessibility.
TanStack Query for data caching, React Router for client-side navigation, and a custom gamification engine.
Supabase (PostgreSQL with Row- Level Security), storing all content in five re- lational tables (
) and serving audio from cloud storage.
Because all content is database-driven, new words and stories can be added without redeploying the application. A role-based landing page (Figure
) lets users enter as a child (learning mode) or as a teacher/parent (administrative view).
The platform offers five distinct modules, each ad- dressing a different aspect of language acquisition:
Swipeable bilingual flashcards with native-speaker audio and illustrative im- ages, filterable by vocabulary domain.
The platform is publicly accessible at
and is hosted on Vercel with global CDN distribution. The re- sponsive design supports desktops, tablets, and mobile phones without requiring a native applica- tion.
Figure 3: The Concept Stories module showing illustrated cards and slide-based bilingual narration for educational topics.
Table 1: Content statistics for the current deploy- ment.
and plant growth, narrated in both Toto and English.
tales with cul- tural annotations, emotional-tone descriptions, and full bilingual audio narration.
Multiple-choice questions at two diffi- culty levels (ages 6 to 9 and 10 to 14), reinforcing vocabulary and comprehension.
Songs, traditions, and general- knowledge items about the Toto people and their heritage.
To sustain engagement among younger learners, the platform implements an experience-point (XP) system with exponential level scaling:

Users earn XP by learning words (+10), completing stories (+25), and answering quiz questions (+5 to +50 depending on accuracy). A streak counter tracks consecutive days of activity. Early levels are deliberately easy to reach so that first-time users experience a sense of progress immediately. This design follows established principles from the gamification literature (
summarises the content collected so far. The numbers are modest, reflecting the pilot nature of the project, but every item has been recorded and verified with native speakers.

Our immediate next step is a series of school-based recording campaigns in Totopara, aiming to expand the vocabulary to 500+ entries across additional domains. Beyond data expansion, we plan to inte- grate a reinforcement-learning module that adapts quiz difficulty to each learner’s performance, follow- ing the approach of Ward et al. (

). We are also exploring IoT-enabled kiosks with offline support for community centres where internet access is un- reliable. Once the dataset reaches a sufficient size, we intend to release it under a CC-BY-NC-SA 4.0 licence for use by other researchers and language communities.

Every endangered language that gains even a small digital foothold has a better chance of being passed on to the next generation. With the platform de- scribed in this paper, we have tried to give Toto such a foothold: a place where children can hear their language spoken, learn new words at their own pace, and earn rewards for doing so. The tool is live, the data is real, and the community is in- volved. We hope it can serve as a replicable model for other small language communities facing similar challenges.
We are grateful to the Toto community of Totopara for their warm participation in our fieldwork,

w:r.w:delText: and especially to Dhaniram Toto for his pioneering work on the Toto scrip

w:r.w:t: t. This work was carried out at IIT Kharagpur.
